evals.prompt: We're going after six named enterprise accounts in financial services -- JPMorgan, Goldman Sachs, Citi, BNY Mellon, State Street, and Northern Trust. These are all existing customers on our mid-market plan and we need to land platform-wide expansion deals. Average deal size target is $850K ARR. Total campaign budget is $120,000 over 90 days. I have one content marketer, one demand gen manager, and a shared designer (12 hours/week). Our CRM is HubSpot, sales team is four enterprise AEs. Legal says we cannot use intent data providers that scrape personal data (EU subsidiary constraint). Past ABM with personalized landing pages worked well; generic webinars did not. Primary KPI must tie to pipeline -- I don't want to hear about impressions. Build me the full campaign brief.
evals.expected_output: A complete ABM campaign brief containing: strategy rationale selecting ABM based on named enterprise accounts and expansion motion; audience definition with firmographic and technographic detail for the six named accounts plus buyer personas (economic buyer, technical evaluator, champion); channel mix with per-channel budget allocation summing to exactly $120,000 across phases covering LinkedIn account-targeted ads, personalized direct mail or gifting, executive roundtable or custom event, personalized landing pages, and email sequences; lead magnet tailored to financial services (e.g., custom ROI analysis, maturity assessment, or benchmarking report); sales-marketing integration section specifying enrichment process, MQA-to-SAL scoring criteria, AE assignment per account, engagement sequence, and SLA for response time on engaged contacts; content calendar scoped to one content marketer plus 12 hrs/week design capacity without overcommitting; success metrics with a pipeline-sourced primary KPI (e.g., $X in qualified pipeline from named accounts) and secondary KPIs like engagement depth per account, multi-threaded contacts per account, meetings booked; risk and contingency section with a specific decision gate (e.g., if fewer than 3 accounts showing engagement signals by Week 4, reallocate budget to highest-responding accounts); budget summary with line items summing to $120,000.
expectations: Pipeline generation: primary KPI is a dollar-denominated pipeline target derived from the six named accounts and $850K average deal size (e.g., $X in qualified expansion pipeline) -- NOT impressions, engagement scores, or any metric that cannot be tied to revenue

Budget efficiency: budget allocation includes per-channel line items summing to exactly $120,000, with rationale for why each channel earns its allocation based on the stated constraint that personalized ABM worked and generic webinars did not -- channels with no historical evidence of ROI for this motion are excluded or deprioritized

Alignment: sales-marketing integration section maps each of the four AEs to specific named accounts, defines MQA-to-SAL scoring criteria that trigger AE engagement, specifies a Salesloft/HubSpot sequence for engaged contacts, and sets a measurable SLA for AE response time on hot accounts

Execution velocity: content calendar and production plan stays within the capacity of one content marketer and 12 hrs/week shared design -- each week's deliverables are listed, and no single week exceeds what one writer can produce while maintaining quality on high-value ABM assets

Pipeline generation: campaign brief includes a structured campaign brief with all sections required to brief an execution team -- strategy rationale, audience definition with buyer personas per account, channel mix, lead magnet, sales-marketing integration, content calendar, success metrics, risk/contingency, and budget summary

Alignment: lead magnet is tailored to financial services expansion buyers (e.g., custom ROI analysis showing platform-wide value vs. current mid-market plan, maturity assessment, or peer benchmarking) -- not a generic ebook or industry-agnostic asset

Budget efficiency: risk and contingency section includes a time-bound decision gate with a named metric, a specific week-number threshold, and a prescribed budget reallocation action (e.g., if fewer than 3 accounts showing engagement by Week 4, shift spend to highest-responding accounts) -- protecting the $120K from being spent on an underperforming motion

evals.prompt: I need a thought leadership campaign to establish us as the authority on AI-powered revenue operations for mid-market SaaS companies (200-2,000 employees, $20M-$200M ARR). Goal is top-of-funnel lead generation -- we need 400 net-new MQLs over the next quarter. Budget is $75,000 for 12 weeks. Team: two content marketers, one demand gen specialist, half-time designer. We can do webinars, LinkedIn, email nurture, and content syndication. We cannot do paid search (brand terms are too expensive in our category) or events (no budget for travel). Historical data: our benchmark report last year generated 280 MQLs at $180 CPL -- best campaign we ever ran. Blog content alone has never moved the needle. Sales team is six mid-market AEs using Salesforce with Outreach sequences. Plan the full campaign.
evals.expected_output: A complete thought leadership campaign brief containing: strategy rationale selecting thought leadership based on complex B2B sale, need for trust-building, and mid-market audience that responds to intelligence and frameworks; audience definition with mid-market SaaS firmographics (200-2,000 employees, $20M-$200M ARR), RevOps/Sales Ops/CRO persona detail, and technographic signals (CRM, sales engagement tools); channel mix allocating $75,000 across LinkedIn sponsored content and thought leader ads, content syndication to relevant B2B networks, webinar series (not one-off), and email nurture sequences -- with no paid search or events; lead magnet anchored in original research (e.g., 'State of AI in Revenue Operations' benchmark report) building on the successful historical benchmark campaign, with clear value proposition for the target persona; sales-marketing integration specifying MQL definition and scoring threshold, Salesforce routing rules, Outreach sequence for MQL follow-up, and AE SLA for speed-to-lead; content calendar that leverages two content marketers and half-time designer without overcommitting (e.g., one major research asset, 2-3 webinars, supporting LinkedIn posts and email content); success metrics with 400 MQLs as primary KPI, secondary KPIs like CPL target (benchmark: sub-$188 to beat $75K/400), content engagement rates, webinar attendance and conversion; risk and contingency with decision gate (e.g., if MQL pace is below 100 by Week 4, shift syndication budget to highest-converting channel); budget summary with line items totaling $75,000.
expectations: Pipeline generation: primary KPI is 400 net-new MQLs with an implied CPL target at or below $187.50 ($75K/400) -- the plan benchmarks against the historical $180 CPL from the benchmark report campaign and explains how channel allocation will hit or beat that efficiency

Budget efficiency: channel mix allocates $75,000 with per-channel line items, excludes paid search and events as instructed, and weights spend toward the research-driven content format that historically produced 280 MQLs -- not toward blog content which was explicitly called out as ineffective

Content leverage: lead magnet is anchored in original research (benchmark report, state-of-industry survey, or similar data-driven asset) that builds on the proven benchmark report format, with a clear plan to derive webinar topics, LinkedIn content, email nurture sequences, and syndication assets from that single research pillar

Alignment: sales-marketing integration defines a specific MQL scoring threshold that triggers Salesforce routing to the six mid-market AEs, specifies the Outreach sequence for MQL follow-up, and sets a measurable speed-to-lead SLA -- connecting marketing's 400-MQL target to AE pipeline activity

Execution velocity: content calendar fits within the capacity of two content marketers and a half-time designer without overcommitting -- production milestones are sequenced so the research asset is complete before derivative content begins, and no week exceeds feasible output

Budget efficiency: risk and contingency section includes a time-bound decision gate with a specific MQL pace threshold (e.g., below 100 MQLs by Week 4), a named metric to evaluate, and a prescribed budget reallocation from underperforming to outperforming channels -- preventing quarter-end scramble on a $75K spend

evals.prompt: We're launching a new product -- an AI-powered pipeline forecasting module -- in eight weeks. This is a new SKU for our existing platform, targeting both net-new logos and cross-sell into our install base of 340 accounts. Net-new target: VP Sales and CRO at B2B SaaS companies, 500-5,000 employees. Cross-sell target: existing customers currently on our CRM analytics plan. Total GTM budget is $200,000 for the launch quarter (12 weeks from announcement to end of launch push). Team: one product marketer, two content marketers, one demand gen manager, full-time designer, and a part-time video editor (20 hrs/week). Channels available: LinkedIn, Google Ads (we have brand terms cleared), email to install base, webinar, partner co-marketing with two integration partners (Salesforce and HubSpot ecosystem), and our owned blog/podcast. We need 60 qualified demos booked in the launch quarter, with at least 20 from net-new logos. Sales team: eight AEs (four hunting, four farming) on Salesforce with Salesloft. Our CEO wants a splashy launch moment but I also need sustained demand gen through the quarter -- not just a spike. Past product launches that were announcement-only fizzled after two weeks. Build the full GTM campaign brief.
evals.expected_output: A complete product launch GTM campaign brief containing: strategy rationale combining product-led awareness burst with sustained demand generation, acknowledging the dual audience (net-new and cross-sell) and the explicit need to avoid announcement-only spike; audience definition split into two segments -- net-new (VP Sales/CRO at B2B SaaS, 500-5,000 employees) and cross-sell (340 existing accounts on CRM analytics plan) -- with persona detail for each; channel mix allocating $200,000 across phased launch (pre-launch tease, launch week burst, sustained demand gen) covering LinkedIn ads, Google Ads on cleared brand terms, email campaigns to install base, webinar or live demo event, partner co-marketing with Salesforce and HubSpot ecosystem partners, blog content, and podcast episodes -- with budget weighted toward sustained demand gen not just launch week; lead magnet appropriate for a product launch (e.g., interactive demo, ROI calculator, early access program, or competitive comparison); sales-marketing integration specifying demo request routing between hunting AEs (net-new) and farming AEs (cross-sell), Salesloft sequence for demo follow-up, scoring model distinguishing net-new from cross-sell leads, and SLA for demo scheduling; content calendar that is feasible for the team (one PM, two content marketers, full-time designer, 20 hrs/week video) across 12 weeks without production gaps or overcommitment; success metrics with 60 qualified demos as primary KPI (with 20 net-new sub-target), secondary KPIs like demo-to-opportunity conversion, cross-sell attach rate, partner-sourced pipeline; risk and contingency addressing the specific risk of post-launch demand dropoff with decision gate (e.g., if demo pace below 15 by Week 4, activate paid retargeting reserve or accelerate partner co-marketing); budget summary with line items totaling $200,000.
expectations: Pipeline generation: primary KPI is 60 qualified demos booked with a 20 net-new sub-target, and the plan includes secondary conversion metrics (demo-to-opportunity rate, cross-sell attach rate) that connect demos to actual pipeline dollars -- not product signups, page views, or announcement impressions

Budget efficiency: $200,000 budget is allocated across phased spend (pre-launch, launch week, sustained demand gen) with the majority weighted toward sustained demand gen weeks 3-12 rather than front-loaded into launch week -- directly addressing the stated risk that announcement-only launches fizzle after two weeks

Alignment: sales-marketing integration routes demo requests to hunting AEs for net-new logos and farming AEs for cross-sell, with a scoring model that distinguishes the two lead types, a Salesloft sequence configured for demo follow-up, and an SLA for demo scheduling turnaround -- ensuring all 60 demos reach the right AE within a defined timeframe

Content leverage: campaign brief treats net-new and cross-sell as distinct audience segments with differentiated messaging, channel selection, and conversion paths -- the cross-sell motion leverages the install base relationship and CRM analytics usage data, while the net-new motion uses ICP-targeted outbound channels

Execution velocity: content calendar sequences deliverables across 12 weeks for the full team (one PM, two content marketers, full-time designer, 20 hrs/week video editor) with no week exceeding team capacity, and production milestones ensure launch-week assets are complete before the launch date with buffer time built in

Pipeline generation: partner co-marketing with Salesforce and HubSpot ecosystem partners is structured as a pipeline-contributing channel with its own demo target or attribution model, not just a logo-placement exercise -- partner-sourced pipeline is tracked as a named secondary KPI

Budget efficiency: risk and contingency section addresses the specific post-launch demand dropoff risk with a time-bound decision gate (e.g., if demo pace below 15 by Week 4), a named metric to evaluate, and a prescribed corrective action (budget reallocation, retargeting activation, or partner acceleration) -- protecting $200K from being spent on a declining-returns curve
